parentQuestion: In a person who is color-blind, which of the following would you most expect to see?

scaffolds.q: Color blindness means difficulty distinguishing certain colors.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Most commonly red-green color blindness - confusing reds and greens.

scaffolds.q: Normal color vision requires all THREE cone types (S, M, L) working properly.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Missing or dysfunctional cone type = missing a comparison channel = color blindness.

scaffolds.q: Red-green color blindness involves problems with M-cones (green) or L-cones (red).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Deuteranopia = no M-cones. Protanopia = no L-cones.

scaffolds.q: The genes for M and L opsins are on the X chromosome.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Males (XY) have only one copy - no backup if it's defective.

scaffolds.q: Color blindness is more common in males because of X-linked inheritance.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! ~8% of males vs ~0.5% of females are red-green color-blind.

scaffolds.q: Color-blind individuals are missing or have defective cones in their foveae.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The fovea is cone-dense - missing cone types here causes color confusion.

scaffolds.q: Rods are NOT affected in typical color blindness.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Rods are achromatic anyway. Color blindness is a CONE problem.

scaffolds.q: Color blindness is due to absence or dysfunction of specific cone types.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Missing green (M) or red (L) cones prevents distinguishing those wavelengths.

scaffolds.q: The answer is 'absence of green or red cones in the foveae.'
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not retinal regeneration (would affect ALL vision), not rods (achromatic), not peripheral vision. Missing cone types = color blindness.
